title: Inspiring Journeys: Kiran Mazumdar-Shaw
text:
[Music]
so this is a special session now called
inspiring Journeys we've had these in
London we've had these in Dubai uh and
it's a moment when we dive into an
entrepreneurial Journey which somebody
with some that we really want you to
meet uh and today we have a special
guest for you our guest is executive
chair of biocon Kieran mazumdar Shaw why
don't you come on
[Applause]
up happy International Women's Day to
you too please last
night so to everyone for that matter
what's that I said happy International
women's 's Day to everyone
exactly so you were India's first
billionaire woman entrepreneur founding
biocon India's first biotech
company and this you've done you did
this at a time when women's
entrepreneurship not only in India but
in a lot of places in the world uh was
at a shall we say a nent stage can you
give me a little flavor about what that
was
like well you know I set up U my company
biocon in
1978 in a country that was very
underresourced
um it was at the height of what we used
to call the license Raj because you
needed permits and permissions for every
single thing and of course it was a time
when entrepreneurship was almost
non-existent in the context of what we
are discussing today there was no
Venture Capital it was all debt
financing and
I was a 25-year-old woman trying to set
up a business with 10,000 rupees in my
bank
account and trying to create a new
business based on biotechnology which
nobody understood so I had huge uh
credibility challenges and I was
considered high risk because I was too
young to be an entrepreneur in the minds
of the people who are going to lend to
me I was considered to be a high
Financial Risk because I had no
collateral to offer I had no business
experience I had no sugar
daddy and and I also was trying to do
something which they thought was too
daunting for a young girl to take on
which was about starting a biotech
business which they themselves couldn't
understand so enormous credibility
challenges and of course u i overcame
them purely through sort of a sense of
conviction and purpose and I had to you
know really make them understand what I
was doing tell us about that take us
into one of those boardrooms and what
was it like as you sat there faced with
I'm assuming men uh asking you these
questions as a young person what were
some of those conversations like oh they
were very very uh some of them were very
difficult conversations and some of of
them were very interesting conversations
so the difficult conversations were give
us two reasons why we should uh you know
give you a loan you any money um what
collateral can you you know provide us
uh can your father provide a
guarantee and I said my father has
nothing to do with my business okay so I
can give you a guarantee but they didn't
want mine okay I said well you accept
personal guarantees from every other
founder or entrepreneur Why Not Me is it
because I'm a woman so I would take them
on headon you know um I of course was
trying to explain the business to them I
was telling them how exciting it was
they said you know why are you trying to
develop a homegrown technology which is
so risky you don't know whether it's
going to work I said well that's what
biotech is all about no why don't you
license a technology they were very used
to licensing a technology and you know
just basically uh manufacturing
something out of it and that was the
engineering licensing technologies that
they were very used to I said no this is
biotech you've got to actually have a
research capability you've got to be
able to do research you've got to be
Innovative you know I had to make them
understand what it was so fortunately um
I did get some people who believed my uh
me believed in me and invested in me and
so I think uh I quickly realized that it
was a case
of you know clearly articulating what
you were trying to do to the people who
are going to lend you money and they
were all men of course right um and I
think I wanted to make myself um in
their eyes somebody worth investing in
and I met a few of those people who felt
I was worth investing in that's what
turned my destiny if I hadn't found
those few men who wanted to who believed
in investing in me I don't think it
would have been that easy and it was all
debt financing you know interest at 16%
those days wow so it wasn't easy and
apart from access to Capital it was also
access to Talent MH people didn't want
to work for a woman employer because
they felt it I was not able to give them
job security so even today when I look
at the the the the situation that me
women entrepreneurs are
uh facing you know as we speak I think
access to Capital Access to talent and
access to technology are some of the
most formidable challenges even
today you know I just want to put right
you know I was at a one of these women's
day discussions yesterday and I said you
know there was a very interesting
Pointview put forth by one of the
panelists and she says you know in the
venture capital
World a VC's question line uh you know
the line of questioning for a a woman
who's seeking funding and a man who's
seeking funding are extremely unfair the
woman has always asked questions about
tell us the risk of this business if you
get married tell us the risk of this
business if you have kids you know and
these kind of questions whereas the man
is always asked about so tell us what
What the how big this business can be
tell us what uh you know the risks of
this business are so you see the two
questionings that go on between men and
women are very unequitable unfair and
unbalanced women have to be asked
questions about their
business not about you know their gender
related issues right that's where we
start this whole gender inequity is what
I feel so I I always tell my colleagues
so today of course thanks to ESG and you
know the Dei
component I think um I've encouraged HR
and my you know my my managers and
everyone who's involved with improving
the gender balance to make sure that
they really provide equal opportunities
for women who are seeking a job in our
organization it's very
important I hope that Mano is taking
note for his Investors Group the
questions not to ask the
75 women leaders well these are
questions these are questions even HR Us
in most
companies you know which is which is
where the problem starts I mean I can
tell you that it's not just women
entrepreneurs too that's what I me right
I mean it's uh people ask them of women
mountain climbers you know why are you
mountain climbing when you have children
with the question that they never ask a
mail Mount climber we'll leave that one
for now
but tell me about uh biocon has a huge
presence not just in India also in the
west and you have faced uh you've really
successfully created um competition
against some of the western
pharmaceutical companies not a feat for
the faint-hearted tell us about the
resilience and perseverance that that
takes but give me some specific examples
of of what that looks like so I think
the best example of uh
you know risk taking that I have
personally you know taken and again I go
back to this this busting myths that
women don't take enough risk they're not
ambitious enough they don't think big
and I think I busted those myths if I
may say so um when I think about the
risk I took when I got into this whole
field of or the segment of biosimilar
medicines
the biosimilar pathway had not even been
scripted in the US and European
regulatory
systems and I was going to be making
huge investments in developing bio
similars that will finally get me the
big return that I was seeking for my
investments from the US and advanced
markets and I thought that was a big
risk but what I did was I said to my
colleagues they thought I was taking too
much of a risk for a small company like
ours and I said no let's follow the
science if we follow the science I'm
sure we will align with the regulatory
guidelines and sure enough it worked out
that way and we had many firsts to our
credit we were the first company to be
approved by usfda for two of our
biosimilar drugs and we were the first
company globally again to get an
interchangeable label for our insulin by
us FDA so I think we did pretty well you
know and I I in hindsight I felt we took
a risk but we took a calculated risk
which worked out um and I think that's
what really in hindsight makes me feel
that you know I I played a good
leadership role and even though I got
absolutely uh you know uh trolled bashed
whatever you might say for taking that
decision of moving into biosimilars and
invest in so much R&D dollars into such
a risky business so I can tell you my
stock price didn't move for many years
right and I was how many years oh almost
3 years three or four years and people
were constantly kind of you know
accusing me and alleging that I don't
understand what business is all about
and that you know you're putting your
shareholders money at risk and I would
say look please don't invest in my
company if you're so
faint-hearted
uh so you know I've been through those
times but of course once you start
delivering so it is about credibility
building you know everything is about
building credibility whether it's about
building credibility as a woman whether
it's building credibility as a woman
entrepreneur whether it is building
credibility as a biotech entrepreneur
ultimately the moment you start showing
signs of success I think that glass
barrier or any barrier disappears and
that's something I want to say to all
women here that the glass ceiling
disappears when you start demonstrating
credible
success tell me
about your thoughts on inclusion
technology uh inclusive technology uh
can really
help especially when done mindfully and
I'd love to hear about your thoughts uh
in the healthc care sector
especially first and foremost let me
start by saying
that a gender balanced
economy is a smart economy because it is
smart and
sustainable and I really believe that
without the inclusion of women in the
economic mainstream or the economy in
general none of the sdg goals will be
accomplished and I really mean that I
think women have a big role to play in
delivering on our
SGS and that's why I think you need to
be inclusive that's why and you have to
be inclusive across the board you know I
think technology is a great
Transformer and I think women have to be
part of that digital transformation if
you want to see that change one thing I
do believe is that women are
change influencers and change makers you
can see we don't come with baggage and
therefore we are daring we are willing
to experiment and we're willing to
explore you can see even in the
political leadership today of all the
women who are playing very strong
political roles including our finance
minister that you heard a while
ago we are willing to listen we're
willing to change if necessary
we're willing to experiment if it
doesn't work we can do something else
but we are not afraid to you know take
on those kind of uh you know challenges
or or experimental uh you know
opportunities I don't find men being
able to do that so easily because
they're very rigid they're very worried
they you know I really find men are
extremely insecure when it comes to
Bringing about change
what will happen to me what's going to
happen to the business you know I don't
think men are as big Risk Takers as
women for that
M who are your role models who do you
look up to you know I uh I look up to
people who inspire me and there are a
number of men and women who inspire me
all the time like right from my late
father who inspired me and pushed me
into doing what what I am
today um to my colleagues at work to
entrepreneurs to unicorns to you know I
get so energized in in the city of
bangaluru because I often sort of hang
around with young unicorns and want to
listen to what is it that's driving them
and what is their business model all
about and it's very energizing for me I
enjoy time with young people I enjoy
time with spending time with startups
and listening to their ideas and helping
them to form their ideas so I think that
I find very inspirational of course you
know there are the usual you know people
who made it big and you can get inspired
from what they've done but I get much
more inspired connecting with people and
you know getting that inspiration from
people who I talk to from people whom I
have you know brainstorming sessions
with so that's the way I get inspired
that's how you get energy and those are
my role models because I feel if I spot
one or two of those people who are
really doing something phenomenal I
think I I draw a lot of inspiration from
them you mentioned uh you get
inspiration from these entrepreneurs
from unicorns there aren't an enormous
amount of uh unicorns led by women not
just in India but across the world so
what are the steps we need to take
you've got uh money in the room to get
more of those so I think uh I don't
think there is a a a case of women not
being able to do certain things it's a
case of giving opportunities to women to
do things and you know um I for one find
that um uh women are beginning to start
uh gaining confidence to start
businesses I know a number of women
who've started businesses endured it for
a long period of time and I hope there's
going to be inflection points where they
can actually catapult into
unicorns um I'm very happy to see that
many many new age companies do have
women co-founders that's a very
interesting uh development of course we
have a few unicorns in India who have
been very bold uh Nika the Nika founder
falguni is one of them but there are
many others and um I I also believe that
over the next decade you will start
seeing more women uh running successful
entrepreneurial companies I I really
believe it's going to happen for many
reasons one is that I think the
government of India for sure and
governments across the
world have
decided to enable and Empower women uh
in a much bigger way than they used to
do in the past in the past it was a lot
of tokenism today I think it is very
serious because they realize that
leaving women out of these very
important
areas is only going to hurt them so
that's why I said you know a a gender
balanced economy is a smart and
sustainable economy and I really believe
that and I know for a fact that um all
all of us who have been
successful are you know influencing
other women to take uh this bold step
and to D Big D to dream and dream big
you know but I still feel that I've seen
a lot of women Le uh you know companies
in in even in the
US um I find that there is still a bias
towards a young woman starting a a
business or a startup company compared
to a young man doing the same job so we
need to remove that bias okay and it's
it's about myths and we've got to bust
it okay what's next for you what are you
most excited about in terms of the
impact you can
make so you know I think every one of us
ought to be driven by a sense of
business purpose and my sense of
business purpose has always been about
making huge impact act on global
Healthcare and I have actually spent my
entire entrepreneurial life in the last
decade and a half to provide affordable
access to life-saving and essential
drugs and in my case I so I I chose uh
you know biologics biosimilars and
complex generics because that's the most
expensive part of healthcare costs and I
thought if I can make a difference there
through Innovation and I felt that India
had that
capability to deliver on affordable
Innovation and so that's my dream of
saying how do I make big impact now that
I'm Global and I've just recently
acquired uh you know my partners
economic interest and created a very
large biosimilar company I hope it's
going to be the biggest one day um and I
want to do it with this in mind Health
Equity to me Health Equity affordable
access is is is my uh business purpose
I'm glad to hear you're achieving the
helping to achieve the sustainable
development goals well thank you so much
for spending time with us here at India
global forum thank you it's been a
pleasure really appreciate
[Music]
it
